metadata.scoring_rule: Portionwise Test: All questions counted. Total /50.0.

Q1a.question: Determine the time of supply in the following cases: (i) GPT & Co. carried out service of cleaning and repairs of tanks in an apartment complex, for which the Apartment Owners' Association showed a payment in cash on 4th April to them against work of this description. The dates of the work are not clear from the records of GPT & Co. GPT & Co. have not issued invoice or entered the payment in its books of account. (ii) Gas is supplied by a pipeline to the recipient. The supply is to be made for a period of one year. Monthly payments are to be made by the recipient as per the contract. The Payments of Rs. 2 lakh made in each month were made on July 5, August 5 and September 5 respectively. (MTP 5 Marks Apr'25)
Q1a.question_text: Determine the time of supply in the following cases: (i) GPT & Co. carried out service of cleaning and repairs of tanks in an apartment complex, for which the Apartment Owners' Association showed a payment in cash on 4th April to them against work of this description. The dates of the work are not clear from the records of GPT & Co. GPT & Co. have not issued invoice or entered the payment in its books of account. (ii) Gas is supplied by a pipeline to the recipient. The supply is to be made for a period of one year. Monthly payments are to be made by the recipient as per the contract. The Payments of Rs. 2 lakh made in each month were made on July 5, August 5 and September 5 respectively. (MTP 5 Marks Apr'25)
Q1a.student_answer:
15-7-26                                GST-TEST 1                          Page No.  1
                                                                                Date 15  7  26

                              [Question 1 (a)]

(i) As per Section 13, the time of supply of services
    is under forward charge is:

  * Date of issue of invoice or date of receipt
    of payment whichever is earlier, if the
    invoice was issued within 30 days from date of
    supply of service

                                    or

  * Date of provision of service or date of receipt
    of payment whichever is earlier, if the
    invoice was not issued within 30 days from
    date of supply of service.

In this case, provisions of Section 13 will not
apply since GPT & Co has not issued an
invoice or entered payment in its books of
accounts. The date of supply of service is also
not available. Therefore time of supply will be
date on which recipient of service shows the
payment in his books of accounts.

Therefore time of supply will be 4th April, date
on which Apartment Owners' Association showed
payment in their books of accounts

(ii) As per Section 12, time of supply of a registered
person making outward supply of goods
is:

* Date of issue of invoice or
* Date on which invoice ought to be
have been issued.

In the case of continious supply of goods,
where successive statements of accounts or
successive payments are made, the time of
supply will be the date of issue of
invoice or for each statement of accounts
or for each such successive payments.
In this case
invoice will be issued on date of
payments of Rs 2 lakhs made on July 5,
August 5 & September 5.

Therefore time of supply will be July 5th,
August 5th & September 5th respectively.
Q1a.model_answer: (i) Section 13(2) of the CGST Act, 2017 stipulates that the time of supply of services shall be the earliest of the following dates, namely:- (a) the date of issue of invoice by the supplier, if the invoice is issued within the period prescribed under section 31 or the date of receipt of payment, whichever is earlier; or (b) the date of provision of service, if the invoice is not issued within the period prescribed under section 31 or the date of receipt of payment, whichever is earlier; or (c) the date on which the recipient shows the receipt of services in his books of account, in a case where above two cases do not apply. In this case, neither the invoice has been issued nor the date of provision of service is available. The date of receipt of payment in the books of the supplier is also not available. Therefore, the time of supply will be the date on which the recipient of service shows receipt of the service in his books of account. Thus, time of supply will be 4th April, the date on which the Apartment Owners' Association records the receipt of service in its books of account. (ii) As per Notification No. 66/2017 CT dated 15.11.2017, a registered person (excluding composition supplier and registered persons making supply of specified actionable claims) has to pay GST on the outward supply of goods at the time of supply as specified in section 12(2)(a) of the CGST Act, 2017, i.e. date of issue of invoice or the last date on which invoice ought to have been issued in terms of section 31 of the CGST Act, 2017. As per section $31(4)$ of the CGST Act, 2017, in case of continuous supply of goods, where successive statements of accounts or successive payments are involved, the invoice is issued before or at the time of each such statement is issued or, as the case may be, each such payment is received. Therefore, invoices should be issued for Rs. 2 lakh each on or before July 5, August 5 and September 5, when monthly payments of Rs. 2 lakh are received. Thus, assuming that the invoice is issued on July 5, August 5 and September 5, the time of supply for the purpose of payment of tax will be July 5, August 5 and September 5 respectively for goods valued at Rs. 2 lakh each.
Q1a.feedback: Excellent understanding of time of supply provisions for both services and goods. You correctly identified the residual rule under Section 13 for part (i) and arrived at the correct answer of 4th April. For part (ii), you correctly applied the continuous supply of goods provisions and identified July 5, August 5, and September 5 as the time of supply. The answer demonstrates strong conceptual clarity. Minor gap: you mentioned 'shows payment' instead of 'shows receipt of services' in books of account for part (i), and didn't explicitly cite Notification No. 66/2017 CT for part (ii), but the core reasoning and conclusions are correct.
major_errors: Minor imprecision: described recipient's entry as 'payment' rather than 'receipt of services' in books of account for part (i)
Did not cite Notification No. 66/2017 CT which is the specific authority for the goods time of supply rule in part (ii)
correct_items: Correctly identified that Section 13 residual rule applies when invoice not issued and payment not recorded in supplier's books
Correctly concluded time of supply as 4th April based on recipient's books of account
Correctly identified continuous supply of goods provisions under Section 12
Correctly determined time of supply as July 5, August 5, and September 5 for each payment of Rs. 2 lakh
Correct application of invoice issuance rules for successive payments

Q1b.question: Mr. Sky is engaged in the business of trading of mobiles. He is eligible for composition scheme and has opted for the same. He seeks your advice for records which are not required to be maintained by him as composition taxable person. (PYP, 4 marks)
Q1b.question_text: Mr. Sky is engaged in the business of trading of mobiles. He is eligible for composition scheme and has opted for the same. He seeks your advice for records which are not required to be maintained by him as composition taxable person. (PYP, 4 marks)
Q1b.student_answer:
[Question 1(b)]
↑
Following records are not required to be
maintained by Ms Sky as composition taxable
person:

(i) Stock of goods

* Accounts of stock of goods received &
   supplied by him
* Accounts will include a opening balance,
  supply, receipt, goods lost or stolen, disposed
  of by way of gift or free samples, destroyed,
  written off etc and closing balance of stock
  which includes raw material, work in progress &
  finished goods.

(ii) Details of tax
  * Accounts, containing details of tax payable
   (including payable under reverse charge)
   tax paid, tax collected, input tax credit
   available, input tax credit availed. It will
   also include a register of tax invoices, debit
   notes & credit notes, delivery challan etc.
Q1b.model_answer:
A supplier who has opted for composition scheme is not required to maintain records relating to;

(a) Stock of goods: Accounts of stock in respect of goods received and supplied by him, and such accounts shall contain particulars of the opening balance, receipt, supply, goods lost stolen, destroyed, written off or disposed of by way of gift or free sample and the balance of stock including raw materials, finished goods, scrap and wastage thereof.

(b) Details of tax: Account, containing the details of tax payable (including tax payable under reverse charge), tax collected and paid, input tax, input tax credit claimed, together with a register of tax invoice, credit notes, debit notes, delivery challan issued or received during any tax period.

Thus, Mr. Sky is not required to maintain above mentioned records.
Q1b.feedback: Excellent answer! You've correctly identified both categories of records not required to be maintained under the composition scheme - stock of goods and details of tax. Your explanation of each category is comprehensive and accurate, covering all the key elements like opening balance, receipts, supplies, lost/stolen goods, gifts/free samples, and the tax-related registers. Minor omission of 'scrap and wastage' in stock details and 'input tax credit claimed' phrasing slightly differs from model, but overall this is a very strong response.
key_points_covered: Composition taxable person not required to maintain certain records
Stock of goods - accounts of stock received and supplied
Opening balance, receipt, supply details
Goods lost, stolen, destroyed, written off, disposed of by gift or free sample
Closing balance including raw materials, finished goods
Details of tax payable including reverse charge
Tax collected and paid
Input tax credit claimed/availed
Register of tax invoices, credit notes, debit notes, delivery challans
key_points_missed: Minor: 'scrap and wastage' not explicitly mentioned in stock details
Minor: 'work in progress' mentioned instead of 'scrap and wastage' - slight deviation but acceptable
major_errors: Minor omission: 'scrap and wastage' not mentioned in stock details (model answer includes these)
Included 'work in progress' which is not in the model answer (though not incorrect conceptually)
correct_items: Correctly identified stock of goods accounts as not required
Covered opening balance, receipt, supply, goods lost/stolen/destroyed/written off/disposed as gift or free sample
Correctly identified details of tax accounts as not required
Covered tax payable including reverse charge, tax collected and paid, input tax credit
Mentioned register of tax invoices, debit notes, credit notes, delivery challans

Q2a.question: Satya Sai Residents Welfare Association, a registered person under GST has 30 members each paying ₹ 8,000 as maintenance charges per month for sourcing of goods and services from third persons for common use of its members. The Association purchased a water pump for ₹ 59,000 (inclusive of GST of ₹ 9,000) and availed input services for ₹ 23,600 (inclusive of GST of ₹ 3,600) for common use of its members during February, 20XX. Compute the total GST payable, if any, by Satya Sai Residents Welfare Association, for February, 20XX. GST rate is 18%. All transactions are intra-state. There is no opening ITC and all conditions for ITC are fulfilled.
Q2a.question_text: Satya Sai Residents Welfare Association, a registered person under GST has 30 members each paying ₹ 8,000 as maintenance charges per month for sourcing of goods and services from third persons for common use of its members. The Association purchased a water pump for ₹ 59,000 (inclusive of GST of ₹ 9,000) and availed input services for ₹ 23,600 (inclusive of GST of ₹ 3,600) for common use of its members during February, 20XX. Compute the total GST payable, if any, by Satya Sai Residents Welfare Association, for February, 20XX. GST rate is 18%. All transactions are intra-state. There is no opening ITC and all conditions for ITC are fulfilled.
Q2a.model_answer:
Computation of total GST payable by Satya Sai Residents Welfare Association for February, 20XX:-

| Particulars | Value (₹) | GST @ 18% |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Maintenance charges received [₹ 8,000×30 members] [Refer Note](It is logically presumed that maintenance charges are exclusive of GST) | 2,40,000 | 43,200 |

Total GST payable | | 43,200 |
Less:- ITC of GST paid on water pump purchased (₹ 9,000) and input services availed (₹ 3,600) | | (12,600) |

Net GST payable | | 30,600 |

Note:- Services by RWA to its members for sourcing of goods or services from a third person for common use of members in a housing society are exempt, if the share of contribution per month per member is upto ₹ 7,500. Otherwise, entire amount is taxable. Thus, maintenance charges are taxable in this case.

Q2b.question: b) Dharun provides service as a business facilitator to Zio Bank Limited by facilitating in opening of accounts to villagers in its rural branches in Punjab and earned a commission of ₹ 22 lakh in the month of April, 20XX. So far, he is not registered under GST. Dharun’s tax consultant advised him that he is not liable for registration under GST as his gross receipts exceeded ₹ 20 lakh. Dharun has no other receipt/business activity other than the above. [CA Inter Nov 22 Exam]
Q2b.question_text: b) Dharun provides service as a business facilitator to Zio Bank Limited by facilitating in opening of accounts to villagers in its rural branches in Punjab and earned a commission of ₹ 22 lakh in the month of April, 20XX. So far, he is not registered under GST. Dharun’s tax consultant advised him that he is not liable for registration under GST as his gross receipts exceeded ₹ 20 lakh. Dharun has no other receipt/business activity other than the above. [CA Inter Nov 22 Exam]
Q2b.student_answer:
[Question 2(b)]
As per the provisions of Section 22, a supplies
is liable to obtain registration if his aggregate
& turnover exceeds the threshold limit i.e
₹10 lakhs for special category states, being
Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura & Nagaland & ₹20 lakhs
for the rest of the states. for supply of services.
However, services provided by a business
facilitator to a banking company in a taxable
territory & in the rural area branch is exempt.

In this case, Dharun is engaged exclusively in supply
of exempt services.

Therefore even though, his aggregate turnover
is exceeding the threshold limit,
he is not liable to obtain registration.

Thus advice given by the tax consultant is
partially correct, regarding Dharun not
being liable to registration.
Q2b.model_answer: b) Services provided by business facilitator to a banking company with respect to accounts in its rural branch are exempt from GST. In the given case, Dharun is engaged exclusively in providing the exempt services, it is not liable to obtain registration even though his aggregate turnover exceeds ₹ 20 lakh. Thus, the advice given by his tax consultant is not correct.
Q2b.feedback: Very good answer! You correctly identified the key exemption for business facilitator services to banking companies in rural branches, and correctly concluded that Dharun is not liable for registration despite exceeding the threshold limit. Your explanation of the threshold limits under Section 22 adds good context. The only minor issue is your conclusion that the advice is 'partially correct' — the model answer states the advice is 'not correct' because the consultant's reasoning was wrong (the consultant cited the ₹20 lakh threshold as the reason, whereas the actual reason for non-registration is the exempt nature of the services, not the threshold). Overall, a strong and well-structured answer!
key_points_covered: Services provided by business facilitator to banking company in rural branch are exempt from GST
Dharun is engaged exclusively in supply of exempt services
Not liable to obtain registration even though aggregate turnover exceeds threshold limit
Threshold limit of ₹20 lakh mentioned
Correct conclusion that Dharun is not liable for registration
key_points_missed: The advice of tax consultant is 'not correct' (student says 'partially correct' — the reason given by consultant was wrong even if conclusion was right)
major_errors: Conclusion that the tax consultant's advice is 'partially correct' is inaccurate — the advice is not correct because the consultant cited the wrong reason (threshold limit) rather than the actual reason (exempt services)
correct_items: Correctly identified the exemption for business facilitator services to banking companies in rural branches
Correctly applied Section 22 threshold limits
Correctly concluded Dharun is not liable for registration
Recognized that exempt services affect registration liability

Q3a.question: Mr. X, a registered person has caused movement of goods of consignment value exceeding ₹ 50,000 in relation to a supply and thus, generated e-way bill. However, after generation of e-way bill, he found a mistake in the e-way bill and wants to edit it. You are required to advise Mr. X whether he can do so with the help of relevant provisions? [Study Mat] [CA Inter RTP Nov 23] [CA Inter MTP Sep 23]
Q3a.question_text: Mr. X, a registered person has caused movement of goods of consignment value exceeding ₹ 50,000 in relation to a supply and thus, generated e-way bill. However, after generation of e-way bill, he found a mistake in the e-way bill and wants to edit it. You are required to advise Mr. X whether he can do so with the help of relevant provisions? [Study Mat] [CA Inter RTP Nov 23] [CA Inter MTP Sep 23]
Q3a.student_answer:
[Question 3(a)]
In case of any omission, or mistake, made in
the eway bill, such eway bill cannot be
edited or modified, once generated. The only
option will be cancellation of such eway bill
within 24 hours of generation of such eway bill
and generation of a new eway bill with
the correct details.
However, such eway bill can be updated if the
mistake was of vehicle number.

Therefore Mr X cannot edit or modify the eway
bill once it has been generated. He will have
to cancel such eway bill within 24 hours of such
generation and issue a new eway bill.
Q3a.model_answer: If there is a mistake, incorrect or wrong entry in the e-way bill, then it cannot be edited or corrected. The only option is cancellation of e-way bill within 24 hours of generation and generate a new one with correct details. Thus, Mr. X cannot edit the e-way bill. However, he can cancel the e-way bill within 24 hours of generation and generate a new one with correct details.
Q3a.feedback: Excellent answer! You've correctly identified that e-way bills cannot be edited or modified once generated, and that the only option is cancellation within 24 hours followed by generation of a new e-way bill. You also added a valuable additional point about vehicle number updates being permitted, which shows deeper knowledge of the provisions. This is a well-structured and complete response.
key_points_covered: Cannot be edited or corrected once generated
Cancellation within 24 hours of generation
Generate a new e-way bill with correct details
Correct conclusion: Mr. X cannot edit the e-way bill
Additional correct point: vehicle number can be updated
correct_items: Correctly stated that e-way bill cannot be edited or modified once generated
Correctly identified the 24-hour cancellation window
Correctly advised generating a new e-way bill with correct details
Added the additional nuance about vehicle number updates being permissible
Clear practical advice given to Mr. X

Q3b.question:
Determine the time of supply of goods in the following independent cases assuming that GST is payable under reverse charge. [Study Mat]

| S.No. | Date of receipt of goods | Date of payment by recipient of goods | Date of issue of invoice by supplier of goods |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| 1     | July 1                   | August 10                             | June 29                                       |
| 2     | July 1                   | June 25                               | June 29                                       |
| 3     | July 1                   | Part payment made on June 30 & balance amount paid on July 20 | June 29 |
| 4     | July 5                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 28 & debited in his bank account on June 30 | June 1 |
| 5     | July 1                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 30 & debited in his bank account on June 26 | June 29 |
| 6     | August 1                 | August 10                             | June 29                                       |
Q3b.question_text:
Determine the time of supply of goods in the following independent cases assuming that GST is payable under reverse charge. [Study Mat]

| S.No. | Date of receipt of goods | Date of payment by recipient of goods | Date of issue of invoice by supplier of goods |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| 1     | July 1                   | August 10                             | June 29                                       |
| 2     | July 1                   | June 25                               | June 29                                       |
| 3     | July 1                   | Part payment made on June 30 & balance amount paid on July 20 | June 29 |
| 4     | July 5                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 28 & debited in his bank account on June 30 | June 1 |
| 5     | July 1                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 30 & debited in his bank account on June 26 | June 29 |
| 6     | August 1                 | August 10                             | June 29                                       |
Q3b.model_answer:
Answer :- Legal Provision:- The TOS of goods on which GST is payable on reverse charge basis, shall be determined on the basis of following dates; [See 12(3)]

a) Goods Receipt Date: The date on which the goods are received.

b) Payment Recording Date: Either when the payment is recorded in the recipient's books of account or when it's debited in his bank account, whichever is earlier.

c) 31st day from Invoice Date : The day immediately after 30 days from the supplier's invoice issue date or its equivalent documents

Determination of time of supply of goods under reverse charge:-

| S.No. | Date of receipt of goods | Date of payment by recipient of goods | Date of issue of invoice by supplier of goods | 31st day from date of Invoice | Time of Supply of Goods u/s 12(3) (Earlier of (1),(2) & (3)) |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1     | July 1                   | August 10                             | June 29                                      | July 30                       | July 1                                                      |
| 2     | July 1                   | June 25                               | June 29                                      | July 30                       | June 25                                                    |
| 3     | July 1                   | Part payment made on June 30 & balance amount paid on July 20 | June 29 | July 30 | June 30 for part payment & July 20 for balance amount |
| 4     | July 5                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 28 & debited in his bank account on June 30 | June 1 | July 2 | June 28 |
| 5     | July 1                   | Payment entered in recipient's books of account on June 30 & debited in his bank account on June 26 | June 29 | July 30 | June 26 |
| 6     | August 1                 | August 10                             | June 29                                      | July 30                       | July 30                                                    |
Q3b.feedback: You indicated the question was incomplete rather than attempting a solution. The question was actually complete and required determining the time of supply under reverse charge for 6 independent cases. Try to attempt questions even if you feel information might be missing - partial credit is always available for correct methodology.
major_errors: No attempt made at solving any of the 6 cases
Failed to apply Section 12(3) provisions for time of supply under reverse charge
Did not identify the three key dates: receipt of goods, payment date, and 31st day from invoice
correct_items: No calculations or logic steps were attempted

Q4a.question:
Compute the Gross value of supplies, on which GST to be paid by Mr. Jayesh for the month of October. (PYP 6 Marks, May'23)

Mr. Jayesh, a registered person under GST, has furnished the following details for the month of October:

| Particulars | Amount (Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Commission received as a recovery agent from Non-Banking Financial Company | 45,000 |
| Actionable claim received from normal business debtors | 1,20,000 |
| Amount received from ABC Ltd. for performance of classical dance | 1,74,500 |
| Business assets given free of cost (ITC was not availed on such assets) | 85,000 |
| Rent received from a registered individual person for use of his residential dwelling | 22,000 |
| Services availed from an unregistered Goods Transport Agency (GTA) | 15,100 |
Q4a.question_text:
Compute the Gross value of supplies, on which GST to be paid by Mr. Jayesh for the month of October. (PYP 6 Marks, May'23)

Mr. Jayesh, a registered person under GST, has furnished the following details for the month of October:

| Particulars | Amount (Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Commission received as a recovery agent from Non-Banking Financial Company | 45,000 |
| Actionable claim received from normal business debtors | 1,20,000 |
| Amount received from ABC Ltd. for performance of classical dance | 1,74,500 |
| Business assets given free of cost (ITC was not availed on such assets) | 85,000 |
| Rent received from a registered individual person for use of his residential dwelling | 22,000 |
| Services availed from an unregistered Goods Transport Agency (GTA) | 15,100 |
Q4a.model_answer:
Computation of gross value of taxable supply on which GST is to be paid by Mr. Jayesh

| Particulars | Amount (Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Commission received as a recovery agent from Non-Banking Financial Company [Tax is payable by NBFC under reverse charge.] | - |
| Actionable claim received from normal business debtors [No tax is payable as actionable claims other than lottery, betting and gambling are covered under Schedule III, i.e. they are neither supply of goods nor supply of services.] | - |
| Amount received from ABC Ltd. for performance of classical dance [Taxable since consideration for classical dance performance exceeds Rs. 1,50,000.] | 1,74,500 |
| Business assets given free of cost [Not a supply as it is made without consideration and not covered in Schedule I because ITC is not availed on the same.] | - |
| Rent from registered individual person [Exempt Supply] | - |
| Services from unregistered GTA [Tax on services provided by unregistered GTA is payable under reverse charge by Mr. Jayesh being a registered person.] | 15,100 |
| Gross value of taxable supply on which GST is to be paid by Mr. Jayesh | 1,89,600 |
Q4a.feedback: The student indicated the question was incomplete and did not attempt any computation. No marks can be awarded for content, but a minimal token mark is given for at least acknowledging the question exists. For future attempts, remember to work through each item systematically: check RCM applicability, Schedule III exemptions, and threshold limits for cultural performances.
major_errors: No attempt made at the computation
Student claimed question was incomplete rather than attempting the answer

All six line items left unaddressed — classical dance performance (Rs. 1,74,500), RCM on GTA (Rs. 15,100), and exclusions for recovery agent commission, actionable claims, free assets, and residential rent

Q4b.question: With reference to GST law, Determine the place of supply with reasons in the following independent circumstances: - I. Miss Kanika of Kolkata (West Bengal) visited to Jodhpur Law University (Rajasthan) and paid her college fees by purchasing a demand draft from a bank located in the University campus. Miss Kanika did not have any account with the bank. II. Mizu Machine Ltd., registered in the State of Andhra Pradesh, supplied a machinery to Keyan Wind Farms Ltd., registered in the State of Karnataka. However, this machinery was assembled and installed at the wind mill of Keyan Wind Farms Ltd., which was located in the State of Tamil Nadu. (RTP May'24)
Q4b.question_text: With reference to GST law, Determine the place of supply with reasons in the following independent circumstances: - I. Miss Kanika of Kolkata (West Bengal) visited to Jodhpur Law University (Rajasthan) and paid her college fees by purchasing a demand draft from a bank located in the University campus. Miss Kanika did not have any account with the bank. II. Mizu Machine Ltd., registered in the State of Andhra Pradesh, supplied a machinery to Keyan Wind Farms Ltd., registered in the State of Karnataka. However, this machinery was assembled and installed at the wind mill of Keyan Wind Farms Ltd., which was located in the State of Tamil Nadu. (RTP May'24)
Q4b.student_answer:
[Question 4(b)]

1) In the case of banking & financial services
place of supply will be location of the
receipient as per the records of the supplier.
However, if location of receipient is not given
the place of supply will be location of
the supplier of such services.

In this case, since Kanika's location is not in
the bank's record, the place of supply will
be the location of the bank.

Therefore place of supply is Jodhpur, Rajasthan

2) Where the goods supplied are to be
installed and assembled at site, place
of supply will be the location of such
installation or assembly.

Therefore place of supply is Tamil Nadu.
Q4b.model_answer: (i) Section 12(12) of the IGST Act, 2017 provides that the place of supply of banking and other financial services, including stock broking services to any person is the location of the recipient of services in the records of the supplier of services. However, if the location of recipient of services is not available in the records of the supplier, the place of supply is the location of the supplier of services. Therefore, since the location of recipient is not available in the records of the supplier, the place of supply is the location of the supplier of services, i.e. Rajasthan (or Jodhpur). (ii) Section 10(1)(d) of the IGST Act, 2017 provides that if the supply involves goods which are to be installed or assembled at site, the place of supply is the place of such installation or assembly. Thus, the place of supply is the site of assembly of machine, i.e. Tamil Nadu.
Q4b.feedback: Excellent answer! Both parts are correctly addressed with proper legal reasoning. You correctly identified the applicable provisions for banking services and installation/assembly of goods, and arrived at the correct place of supply for both scenarios (Rajasthan/Jodhpur and Tamil Nadu respectively). The explanation is clear and well-structured.
correct_items: Correctly identified Section 12(12) principle for banking services - location of recipient in supplier's records
Correctly applied the exception - when recipient's location not available, place of supply is supplier's location
Correctly concluded place of supply as Jodhpur, Rajasthan for Part I
Correctly identified the installation/assembly rule for goods
Correctly concluded place of supply as Tamil Nadu for Part II

Q5a.question:
Question 8 (PYP 6 Marks, Jul'21,MP[4]) (Same concepts different figures MTP 5 Marks Aug'24)

A Ltd. procured the following goods in the month of December.

| Inward Supplies | GST (Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- |
| (1) Goods used in constructing an additional floor of office building | 18,450 |
| (2) Goods given as free sample to prospective customers | 15,000 |
| (3) Trucks used for transportation of inputs in the factory | 11,000 |
| (4) Inputs used in trial runs | 9,850 |
| (5) Confectionery items for consumption of employees working in the factory | 3,250 |
| (6) Cement used for making foundation and structural support to plant and machinery | 8,050 |

Compute the amount of ITC available with A Ltd. for the month of December by giving necessary explanations. Assume that all the other conditions necessary for availing ITC have been fulfilled.
Q5a.question_text:
Question 8 (PYP 6 Marks, Jul'21,MP[4]) (Same concepts different figures MTP 5 Marks Aug'24)

A Ltd. procured the following goods in the month of December.

| Inward Supplies | GST (Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- |
| (1) Goods used in constructing an additional floor of office building | 18,450 |
| (2) Goods given as free sample to prospective customers | 15,000 |
| (3) Trucks used for transportation of inputs in the factory | 11,000 |
| (4) Inputs used in trial runs | 9,850 |
| (5) Confectionery items for consumption of employees working in the factory | 3,250 |
| (6) Cement used for making foundation and structural support to plant and machinery | 8,050 |

Compute the amount of ITC available with A Ltd. for the month of December by giving necessary explanations. Assume that all the other conditions necessary for availing ITC have been fulfilled.

(ITC on goods disposed off by way of

3) Trucks used for transportation                    11,000

(ITC on motor vehicles used for transport

Q5a.model_answer:
Computation of amount of ITC available for the month of December

| S. No. | Particulars | GST(Rs.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (1) | Goods used in construction of additional floor of office building [ITC on goods received by a taxable person for construction of an immovable property on his own account is blocked even if the same is used in the course or furtherance of business. It has been assumed that cost of construction of additional floor has been capitalized.] | Nil |
| (2) | Goods given as free samples to prospective customers [ITC on goods disposed of by way of free samples is blocked.] | Nil |
| (3) | Trucks used for transportation of inputs in the factory [ITC on motor vehicles used for transportation of goods is not blocked ${ }^{1}$.] | 11,000 |
| (4) | Inputs used in trial runs [Being used in trial runs, inputs are used in the course or furtherance of business and hence ITC thereon is allowed.] | 9,850 |
| (5) | Confectionary items for consumption of employees working in the factory [ITC on food or beverages is blocked unless the same is used in same line of business or as an element of the taxable composite or mixed supply. Further, ITC on goods and/or service used for personal consumption is blocked.] | Nil |
| (6) | Cement used for making foundation and structural support to plant and machinery [ITC on goods used for construction of plant and machinery is not blocked. Plant and machinery includes foundation and structural supports through which the same is fixed to earth.] | 8,050 |
|  | Total eligible ITC | 28,900 |

${ }^{1}$ It has been assumed that depreciation has not been claimed on tax component.

Your explanations for blocked credits (immovable property construction, free samples, food/beverages for personal consumption) and eligible credits (motor vehicles for goods transport, trial run inputs, plant & machinery foundation) are all accurate and well-articulated.

Q5b.question: Examine whether the following activities would be treated as supply under GST law? (PYP 4 Marks Nov'23) (i) Mr. Sonu from Chandigarh purchased a water cooler from Malhotra Bros. of Hoshiarpur for Rs.25,000 to donate it to a temple situated in Hoshiarpur. Mr. Sonu directed Malhotra Bros. to engrave the words on the water cooler- "Donated by Mr. Sonu from Chandigarh" and dispatch the water cooler directly to the temple. (ii) Wiwitsu Ltd, a registered person in Ahmedabad (Gujarat) having head office located in Singapore, received management consultancy services free of cost from its head office.
Q5b.question_text: Examine whether the following activities would be treated as supply under GST law? (PYP 4 Marks Nov'23) (i) Mr. Sonu from Chandigarh purchased a water cooler from Malhotra Bros. of Hoshiarpur for Rs.25,000 to donate it to a temple situated in Hoshiarpur. Mr. Sonu directed Malhotra Bros. to engrave the words on the water cooler- "Donated by Mr. Sonu from Chandigarh" and dispatch the water cooler directly to the temple. (ii) Wiwitsu Ltd, a registered person in Ahmedabad (Gujarat) having head office located in Singapore, received management consultancy services free of cost from its head office.
Q5b.student_answer:
[Question 5(b)]

(i) → Supply of water cooler from Malhotra Bros
    to Mr Sonu will be supply since it is made
    for a consideration in the course or furtherance
    of business

→ Supply of water cooler from Mr Sonu to the
   temple:
   Donation received by charitable organisations will
   be considered as consideration only when there
   is 'quid pro quo' i.e something in return.
   There should be no obligation on the part of

the recipient to do anything for such
donation.
However, if the charitable organisation
displays the name of the donor in its
premises as an expression of gratitude and
public recognition of the donor's act of
philanthropy and is not any advertising or
promotion of donor's business, it will not
be considered as supply under GST law.

Therefore, donation of water cooler by Sonu
to the temple does not have 'quid pro quo'
and will not be treated as supply under GST
law.
Q5b.model_answer: (i) Donations received by the charitable organisations are treated as consideration only if there exists, quid pro quo, i.e., there is an obligation on part of recipient of the donation or gift to do anything (supply a service). Since the name of the donor, Mr. Sonu is displayed in temple as an expression of gratitude and public recognition of his act of philanthropy and is not aimed at advertising or promotion of his business, hence, donation of water cooler by Mr. Sonu to temple is without quid pro quo and is, thus, not a supply under GST law. However, supply of water cooler by Malhotra Bros. to Mr. Sonu is supply as it is made for consideration in course or furtherance of business. (ii) As per schedule I, in case of import of services by a person from a related person or from his establishments located outside India, without consideration, in the course or furtherance of business shall be treated as "supply". Hence, services received by Wiwitsu Ltd. qualify as supply even though such services have been provided free of cost by the head office.
Q5b.feedback: Good understanding of both scenarios! You correctly identified the quid pro quo concept for charitable donations and explained why the temple display of donor's name doesn't constitute supply. You also correctly identified Malhotra Bros.' supply as taxable. The only gap is that you missed addressing part (ii) about Wiwitsu Ltd. and the Schedule I provision regarding import of services from related persons/head office outside India without consideration. That second scenario was worth marks, so covering it would have earned you full marks.
key_points_covered: Supply of water cooler by Malhotra Bros to Mr. Sonu is supply for consideration in course/furtherance of business
Quid pro quo concept for charitable donations
No obligation on recipient to do anything for donation
Display of donor's name as expression of gratitude and public recognition
Not aimed at advertising or promotion of business
Donation of water cooler by Sonu to temple is not supply under GST law
key_points_missed: Part (ii) - Wiwitsu Ltd receiving management consultancy services from head office in Singapore not addressed at all
Schedule I - import of services by person from related person/establishment outside India without consideration in course of business treated as supply
major_errors: Completely missed part (ii) regarding Wiwitsu Ltd. - failed to address Schedule I provision on import of services from related persons/establishments outside India without consideration being treated as supply
correct_items: Correctly identified that Malhotra Bros.' supply to Mr. Sonu is a taxable supply for consideration in course of business
Correctly explained the quid pro quo concept for charitable donations
Correctly concluded that temple displaying donor's name as gratitude/philanthropy recognition (not business promotion) does not constitute supply
Correctly concluded donation of water cooler to temple is not supply under GST law
